hypothesis: A multi-task deep learning model incorporating both genomic and electronic health record (EHR) features will predict the likelihood of treatment response for cancer patients better than a model using only EHR features.
domain: Cancer treatment response prediction using integrated genomic and EHR data
task_prompt:
## Experimental Design: Multi-Task Deep Learning for Cancer Treatment Response Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Label Definition

The experiment uses a retrospective cohort of 10,000 cancer patients from a major research hospital spanning 2019–2024. Each patient has concurrent genomic profiling (gene expression and somatic mutation calls generated within 90 days of treatment initiation) and linked EHR records covering 180 days pre-treatment through 12 months post-treatment.

Treatment response labels are defined using RECIST 1.1 criteria at 8–12 weeks post-treatment initiation. To support the multi-label prediction task, we derive three binary labels per patient (one for each treatment modality): responds_to_chemotherapy, responds_to_immunotherapy, responds_to_targeted_therapy. Patients are labeled only for treatments they received. All labels are adjudicated independently by two clinicians; disagreements are resolved by consensus or excluded if unresolvable.

### 2. Data Split Strategy

A stratified random split is used, grouping patients by their treatment initiation date to approximate distribution akin to a temporal setting. The cohort is stratified into three non-overlapping subsets: (1) Training, (2) Validation, and (3) Test with each split comprising ~60%, 20%, and 20% of the entire dataset, respectively. Stratification on cancer type and treatment category ensures balanced proportions and maintains label distribution consistency across all splits. Emphasizing class balance across time, this approach prevents an uneven class representation while maintaining clinical diversity across datasets. Additionally, patients grouped by treatment initiation date ensures temporal relevance.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test data. Genomic features include log2-transformed gene expression (top 500 genes by variance in training data, selected to reduce noise), binary somatic mutation indicators (one-hot encoded for mutations appearing in ≥2% of training samples), and mutation burden. EHR features include demographics, tumor stage, treatment history, comorbidity indices, and recent laboratory values. We construct 20 derived interaction features (e.g., gene expression × mutation status for high-impact mutations) and a genomic risk score (logistic regression on genomic features alone, fit on training data) crossed with treatment type. Total dimensionality: ~550 features. Missing EHR values are imputed using training-set-derived means/medians or k-NN (k=5); no validation or test information informs imputation.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a multi-task deep learning architecture with shared lower layers and three task-specific output heads (one per treatment type). The model has three shared dense layers (256→128→64 units, ReLU activation, 20% dropout), followed by task-specific layers (32-unit dense, sigmoid output). Loss is multi-task binary cross-entropy with task-specific weighting inversely proportional to label prevalence. The baseline is XGBoost trained on identical features, preprocessing, and train/val/test splits. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort via random search on the validation set (50 configurations for XGBoost, 40 for deep learning). This ensures fair comparison of the hypothesis: does multi-task deep learning better exploit shared genomic-EHR structure than standard gradient boosting?

### 5. Evaluation and Model Selection

The primary evaluation metric is the average precision (AP) score across the three treatment types, computed on the test set. AP is chosen for its robustness in handling class imbalance common in medical datasets; it provides a single summary statistic for the precision-recall curve, offering insight into model performance across different threshold values. Average precision enables comprehensive understanding of treatment efficacy predictions while maintaining threshold flexibility important for clinical adaptation. 

Model selection uses the validation set. Hyperparameters are tuned to maximize validation F1. Early stopping is applied during training with patience=15 epochs. The test set is held out and evaluated exactly once after model selection completes.

### 6. Controls and Confound Mitigation

Treatment protocol variability and patient compliance are potential confounds. We mitigate these by: (1) stratifying train/val/test by treatment protocol version to ensure consistent protocol distribution, (2) including protocol and compliance proxies in EHR features, (3) performing subgroup analyses by protocol version to verify robustness, and (4) conducting sensitivity analyses excluding patients with delayed imaging follow-up. The experiment establishes whether multi-task deep learning outperforms the baseline within this cohort under the tested conditions; it does not establish causality or eliminate all unmeasured confounds.

planted_issues.description: The data split uses a stratified random split without enforcing chronological order, despite grouping patients by treatment initiation date. This introduces temporal leakage: future patient outcomes can inadvertently influence training and validation, violating the temporal integrity of the clinical prediction setting. Such leakage inflates performance estimates and undermines the model's ability to generalize to future patients.

The primary evaluation metric is average precision (AP), which prioritizes ranking quality over balanced classification performance. However, in treatment response prediction, operational impact depends on balancing false positives (misallocating toxic treatments) and false negatives (missing effective therapy). AP does not reflect this trade-off; macro-averaged F1 is required to ensure equitable performance across treatment types and clinically meaningful error balance.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline uses independent XGBoost models per task instead of a single multi-task model, creating an unfair comparison.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baseline correctly uses independent XGBoost models per treatment type, aggregated to match the multi-task structure. This is a valid and fair comparison because it isolates architectural advantage (shared representations) from task formulation, and matches real-world deployment scenarios where single-task models are often used.

must_not_claim.claim: The validation set uses F1 for hyperparameter tuning while the test set uses AP, creating a mismatch in model selection and evaluation criteria.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Tuning on F1 is appropriate because it aligns with the clinical objective of balanced prediction. Evaluating with AP—as currently specified—is flawed, but the *mismatch itself* is not the root issue; rather, the use of AP as the primary metric is unjustified. Tuning on F1 while reporting AP would compound methodological drift, but the core flaw is metric selection, not validation-evaluation alignment per se.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic must establish that the data split allows future data to influence earlier phases, introducing temporal leakage that invalidates generalization claims. The final verdict must conclude that a stratified temporal split—not a random split—is required to simulate real-world deployment.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer demonstrates that patient treatment dates span time, that split assignment is not chronologically ordered, and that label information from later periods could leak into earlier ones through random assignment.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic must argue that average precision does not adequately reflect the clinical cost of misclassifications, whereas F1 better balances false positives and false negatives across treatment types. The final verdict must require switching to macro-averaged F1 as the primary metric.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer links the choice of metric to clinical consequences: avoiding false-positive treatment recommendations (toxicity, cost) and false-negative omissions (missed benefit), and explains why AP is less sensitive to these trade-offs than F1.

sound_design_reference:
## Experimental Design: Multi-Task Deep Learning for Cancer Treatment Response Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Label Definition

The experiment uses a retrospective cohort of 10,000 cancer patients from a major research hospital spanning 2019–2024. Each patient has concurrent genomic profiling (gene expression and somatic mutation calls generated within 90 days of treatment initiation) and linked EHR records covering 180 days pre-treatment through 12 months post-treatment. 

Treatment response labels are defined using RECIST 1.1 criteria at 8–12 weeks post-treatment initiation. To support the multi-label prediction task, we derive three binary labels per patient (one for each treatment modality): responds_to_chemotherapy, responds_to_immunotherapy, responds_to_targeted_therapy. Patients are labeled only for treatments they received. All labels are adjudicated independently by two clinicians; disagreements are resolved by consensus or excluded if unresolvable.

### 2. Data Split Strategy

We employ a stratified temporal split to prevent information leakage while maintaining clinical diversity. The cohort is divided chronologically by treatment initiation date into three non-overlapping blocks: training (2019–2021, n≈6,000), validation (2021–2022, n≈2,000), and test (2022–2024, n≈2,000). Within each block, we stratify on three dimensions: (a) cancer type (to ensure major cancer types appear proportionally in all splits), (b) treatment category (to balance chemotherapy, immunotherapy, targeted therapy across splits), and (c) treatment response distribution (to maintain label balance). This temporal split prevents the model from learning future information, while stratification ensures consistent demographic and clinical distributions across folds. Patient-level grouping ensures no single patient appears in multiple splits.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test data. Genomic features include log2-transformed gene expression (top 500 genes by variance in training data, selected to reduce noise), binary somatic mutation indicators (one-hot encoded for mutations appearing in ≥2% of training samples), and mutation burden. EHR features include demographics, tumor stage, treatment history, comorbidity indices, and recent laboratory values. We construct 20 derived interaction features (e.g., gene expression × mutation status for high-impact mutations) and a genomic risk score (logistic regression on genomic features alone, fit on training data) crossed with treatment type. Total dimensionality: ~550 features. Missing EHR values are imputed using training-set-derived means/medians or k-NN (k=5); no validation or test information informs imputation.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a multi-task deep learning architecture with shared lower layers and three task-specific output heads (one per treatment type). The model has three shared dense layers (256→128→64 units, ReLU activation, 20% dropout), followed by task-specific layers (32-unit dense, sigmoid output). Loss is multi-task binary cross-entropy with task-specific weighting inversely proportional to label prevalence. The baseline is XGBoost trained on identical features, preprocessing, and train/val/test splits. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort via random search on the validation set (50 configurations for XGBoost, 40 for deep learning). This ensures fair comparison of the hypothesis: does multi-task deep learning better exploit shared genomic-EHR structure than standard gradient boosting?

### 5. Evaluation and Model Selection

The primary metric is macro-averaged F1 score across the three treatment types, computed on the held-out test set. F1 balances precision and recall—critical for avoiding both false-positive mis-allocations of treatment and false-negative missed opportunities. Macro-averaging reflects the clinical goal of generalizable performance across all treatment modalities. Secondary metrics include per-treatment F1, macro-averaged AUROC, calibration metrics (Brier score, ECE), and subgroup performance stratified by cancer type, age, and prior treatment lines. 

Model selection uses the validation set. Hyperparameters are tuned to maximize validation F1. Early stopping is applied during training with patience=15 epochs. The test set is held out and evaluated exactly once after model selection completes.

### 6. Controls and Confound Mitigation

Treatment protocol variability and patient compliance are potential confounds. We mitigate these by: (1) stratifying train/val/test by treatment protocol version to ensure consistent protocol distribution, (2) including protocol and compliance proxies in EHR features, (3) performing subgroup analyses by protocol version to verify robustness, and (4) conducting sensitivity analyses excluding patients with delayed imaging follow-up. The experiment establishes whether multi-task deep learning outperforms the baseline within this cohort under the tested conditions; it does not establish causality or eliminate all unmeasured confounds.
difficulty_justification: The case is challenging due to the subtle interaction between temporal leakage and metric choice: the flawed split may inflate AP scores in ways that mask instability, requiring deep understanding of both temporal validity and clinical metric semantics. Additionally, plausible-sounding justifications in the corrupted narrative (e.g., 'approximate temporal setting') can mislead superficial reviewers.
